detail.evidence: reapOwnerBeforeRespawn (406-435) issues process.kill(ownerPid,'SIGTERM') (417) and process.kill(ownerPid,'SIGKILL') (425) keyed only on processLiveness; same for the main() pendingBootstrapReapPid reap (1244). targetPid originates from a recorded childPid/ownerPid in a stale lease. The owner lease stores executablePath (332,379) but classifyOwnerLease (457-474) and the respawn reap never compare it.
detail.why: If the OS recycled the recorded PID to an unrelated process between recording and reap, the launcher SIGKILLs an innocent process. It is the one kill-target on the escalation path with no guard stronger than bare PID liveness.
detail.recommendation: Before SIGTERM/SIGKILL of a recorded PID, verify it is still the expected process (compare /proc or `ps` comm/executablePath against the lease's executablePath, or a recorded start-time), and treat a mismatch as already-dead rather than killing.

findings.title: Cap-exhaustion refusal can SIGKILL a heartbeating-alive daemon (reap gate ignores lease heartbeat)

detail.evidence: At cap the server closes the socket with no JSON-RPC reply (socket-server.ts:303-307); probeDaemon reads that as 'dead'/closed-before-reply (launcher-ipc-bridge.cjs:227-229); the N-retry mitigation (launcher-ipc-bridge.cjs:348-357) cannot recover because every retry reconnects into the still-full cap; action:'respawn' (launcher-ipc-bridge.cjs:421-424) reaches reapOwnerBeforeRespawn, whose only liveness gate is processLiveness — a running, heartbeating owner is not 'dead', so it falls through to SIGTERM/SIGKILL (mk-spec-memory-launcher.cjs:759-771). Pre-reap guards check only that the lease pid is unchanged (:807-818), never lastHeartbeatIso/ttlMs, though classifyOwnerLease:433-444 already computes that staleness.
detail.why: The cap raise to 64 is a threshold mitigation of the documented refused==dead indistinguishability (socket-server.ts:18-20), but does not remove it: once 64 clients hold slots, an arriving launcher misreads cap-refusal as death and can kill a healthy daemon serving 64 sessions, aborting in-flight SQLite/FTS work. The retry mitigation was tuned for a busy daemon mid-FTS-merge (which eventually replies); cap exhaustion never lets the daemon reply, so retries don't cover it.
detail.recommendation: Before reaping, require the owner lease to be heartbeat-stale (reuse classifyOwnerLease's lastHeartbeatIso/ttlMs check), not merely pid-alive. A daemon that has heartbeated within its TTL must never be reaped on a socket-probe-only 'dead' verdict, regardless of cap.

findings.title: Liveness probes share the client cap, amplifying false-reap below 64 real sessions

detail.evidence: Every accepted connection — including deep-probe liveness connections — is added to activeSockets and spins up a full createServer() (socket-server.ts:310,328), holding a slot from connect until the probe destroys the socket post-reply (launcher-ipc-bridge.cjs:178,227). The cap check at :303 counts these probe slots identically to durable bridge slots.
detail.why: A synchronized launcher burst (large parallel fan-out probing at once) can transiently saturate the 64-slot cap with probe traffic alone, triggering the false-reap path with far fewer than 64 long-lived sessions. The documented 'cap must exceed realistic session fleet' rationale (socket-server.ts:18-20) reasons only about durable sessions, not probe bursts, so the mitigation's safety margin is smaller than stated.
detail.recommendation: Exempt liveness probes from the client cap (e.g. answer the initialize probe via a lightweight pre-accept path that does not occupy a durable slot, or count probe vs bridge connections separately), so transient probe bursts cannot exhaust the cap intended for real sessions.

findings.title: SPECKIT_*_STATE_DIR does not actually isolate the native CLI lock; comment overstates the guarantee

detail.evidence: fanout-run.cjs:416-419 sets SPECKIT_CODEX_STATE_DIR=<unique stateDir>, but it is never mapped to CODEX_HOME/OPENCODE_HOME/CLAUDE_CODE_HOME and is dropped by buildExecutorDispatchEnv's per-kind prefix allowlist (executor-audit.ts:86-90,551-558), so the native CLI never sees it and writes its real lock to the shared default home. getDefaultStatePaths (executor-audit.ts:184-188) always also scans $HOME/.<home>; detectFromLockfile only matches speckit-guessed lock names (executor-audit.ts:196-199) that nothing in-repo writes, so the layer is inert. The comment 'Isolate per-kind state dirs so same-kind replicas do not collide on lockfiles' is not delivered by this var.
detail.why: A load-bearing safety comment claims same-kind replicas are isolated against lockfile collision via this env var, but the only real isolation comes from the unique lineage.label artifact dir. If a future change made detectFromLockfile match a real CLI lock name in the shared home, concurrent same-kind fan-out would silently self-block (false-positive recursion-guard, recorded as dispatch_failure) — a latent under-fanout risk the comment hides.
detail.recommendation: Either map SPECKIT_<KIND>_STATE_DIR to the CLI's real home env (so the native lock relocates per replica) — weighing the auth-dir tradeoff in executor-audit.ts:78-84 — or rewrite the comment to state the layer is detection-only and that real-lock isolation relies on the CLI's own shared home, removing the false 'do not collide on lockfiles' guarantee.

findings.title: Test named for same-kind-replica lock isolation does not exercise the replica (count>1) path

detail.evidence: The test uses two distinct base labels (alpha, beta) with count:1, not a single label with count>1 that triggers expandLineages's `${label}-${replica}` path (executor-config.ts:333-335). Its assertions (fanout-run.vitest.ts:226-230) only check that two filesystem dirs exist and differ; despite the comment at :186, it never asserts the stub codex's echoed STATE_DIR/LINEAGE_ID values were distinct.
detail.why: The regression guard's name claims it protects same-kind-replica lockfile isolation, but it neither covers the count-expansion code path (the actual same-kind-replica scenario) nor verifies env propagation — so a regression in expandLineages label suffixing or in the extraEnv injection could pass undetected.
detail.recommendation: Add a case with a single executor {label:'rep', kind:'cli-codex', count:2} asserting lineages/rep-1 and lineages/rep-2 each receive a distinct SPECKIT_CODEX_STATE_DIR (assert on the stub's captured stdout, not just dir existence).

findings.title: Same non-exclusive owner-lease reclaim race replicated in skill-advisor and code-index launchers

detail.evidence: mk-skill-advisor-launcher.cjs:502-512 is the identical rename+reread reclaim; checkStrictSingleWriter starts the heartbeat at :723 then calls clearOwnerLeaseFile() on loser/bridge paths :727,:752,:769. mk-code-index-launcher.cjs:475 reclaim + clearOwnerLeaseFile :505 + loser path :975 mirror the pattern.
detail.why: Identical check-then-act ownership race; without the fix, all three daemons share the spurious self-termination / ownership-churn failure mode.
detail.recommendation: Apply the same atomic-reclaim (unlink + O_EXCL CAS) fix to both launchers in lockstep with the spec-memory fix; consider hoisting the owner-lease acquire/clear helpers into .opencode/bin/lib to prevent future divergence.

findings.title: Union semantic matches scope-filtered after max-limit slice, starving scoped recall

detail.evidence: filterSemanticMatchesByScope(semanticDatabase, matchSemanticTriggers(queryEmbedding, semanticCache, { max: limit }), args) — matchSemanticTriggers slices to top `limit` globally-ranked matches (matcher.ts:439) BEFORE scope filtering. The lexical path (handler:424) instead over-fetches `limit * 2` candidates then scope-filters (handler:429-461).
detail.why: For a scoped query, the semantic candidate pool is capped at `limit` global matches before scoping, so in-scope matches ranked just below higher-scored out-of-scope matches are dropped. This degrades scoped/multi-tenant recall and is inconsistent with the C2 lexical over-fetch pattern. It fails closed (no cross-scope leak), so impact is recall reduction only, and only in non-default union mode.
detail.recommendation: Pass a larger or unbounded max to matchSemanticTriggers (e.g. limit*4) and let filterSemanticMatchesByScope reduce to the final scoped set, then slice to limit — mirroring the lexical limit*2 over-fetch.

findings.title: Playbook 'off mode writes no retention state' contradicts baseline-delete behavior

detail.evidence: Doc says 'Off mode is inert' / 'Off mode writes no audit or retention state' and step 3 says 'capture zero retention mutations'; but the off-mode regression test asserts swept:1, deletedIds:[1] (memory-retention-feedback-learning.vitest.ts:109) — baseline TTL deletion still runs when the master flag is off.
detail.why: 'Inert' is true only for the feedback reducer, not the whole sweep. An operator seeding expired rows and following step 3 verbatim would observe baseline deletion and could record a false test failure.
detail.recommendation: Reword to 'Off mode runs no feedback-driven audit or retention decisions; the baseline TTL sweep still deletes expired non-protected rows' and adjust step 3 to expect baseline deletion.

findings.title: Tautological relation guard makes insert_rejected branch dead code

detail.evidence: enabledRelation() returns RELATION_TYPES.ENABLED only if Object.values(RELATION_TYPES) includes it; ENABLED is always a member, so it never returns null and the `if (!relation)` -> 'insert_rejected' branch at line 197-200 can never execute.
detail.why: A guard that reads as a validation gate but can never trip is a maintenance trap; a future reader may assume the relation is validated against runtime data when it is a compile-time constant.
detail.recommendation: Drop the tautological check and return RELATION_TYPES.ENABLED directly, or replace it with a real validation against the live schema/enabled-relation set if dynamic validation was the intent.

findings.title: Reindex detach-before-rename swallows all errors as 'not attached'; no inode backstop in path-string attach check

detail.evidence: detachActiveVectorShard(jobDb) is wrapped in try/catch whose comment states 'Not attached on this connection; the rename is still safe', treating every throw as benign. detachActiveVectorShard (vector-index-store.ts:1664-1674) execs DETACH DATABASE active_vec, which can also throw when the attachment is busy/locked, leaving it LIVE. The only attachment-identity check, get_attached_vector_path (vector-index-store.ts:460-463), reads PRAGMA database_list.file (path string), and the attach fast-path (vector-index-store.ts:1562-1605) compares attachedPath===resolve(shardPath) with no inode comparison.
detail.why: If DETACH ever fails with the attachment still live, fs.renameSync(staging,activeShardPath) at reindex.ts:677 leaves the connection bound to the orphaned old inode while the path string still matches the new file. The re-attach at reindex.ts:737 then hits the path-string fast-path, probes the still-valid OLD shard, passes, and concludes 'already attached'—silently serving stale pre-reindex vectors after the job reports success and flips the active-embedder pointer. There is no inode/file-identity backstop to detect the divergence.
detail.recommendation: After the detach attempt, assert get_attached_vector_path(jobDb)===null before renaming (re-detach or throw otherwise) instead of swallowing all errors as 'not attached'; and/or add a file-identity (fs.statSync ino) check to the attachActiveVectorShard fast-path (vector-index-store.ts:1562) so a path-string match with a changed inode forces a detach+re-attach rather than trusting the stale binding.

findings.title: Packed BM25 numericId slots never recycled — monotonic packedDocIds growth under update churn erodes the bounded-RAM claim

detail.evidence: getPackedNumericId (848-850) only ever does packedDocIds.push() / length++; removePackedDocument (959) sets packedDocIds[numericId]=undefined but no free-list or compaction exists (grep for reuse/recycle/compact returns nothing). Every live update (syncChangedRows:743 -> addDocumentFields:537-539 -> removeDocument + re-add) abandons the old slot and pushes a new one. Only clear()/rebuildFromDatabase (668) resets the array.
detail.why: packedDocIds grows monotonically with cumulative write volume between rebuilds rather than tracking live document count. The legacy in-memory engine keyed off a string Map with no such growth, so the packed engine (new this epic) regresses steady-state memory: the '135MB bounded' RAM claim holds only at fresh warmup, not under sustained re-index/update churn.
detail.recommendation: Recycle freed numericIds via a free-list (push undefined slots onto a stack in removePackedDocument; pop in getPackedNumericId before extending), or document that the RAM bound is warmup-time only and rely on periodic rebuildFromDatabase/daemon re-election to compact. A free-list keeps packedDocIds bounded by live doc count.

findings.title: Conflict detection is structurally unreachable at both call sites

detail.evidence: payload and requestFingerprint are the same idempotencySemantics object (memory-save 3570-3571; memory-crud-update 239-253 structurally identical), so payloadHash always equals requestFingerprintHash; receiptKey is derived from requestFingerprintHash (idempotency-receipts.ts:88-97), so any key match implies a payload match and the conflict branch (idempotency-receipts.ts:136-137) plus markResponseWithReceiptStoreConflict can never fire.
detail.why: The documented 'idempotency key conflict' protection and the force-in-key justification at memory-save.ts:3562-3565 describe an event that cannot occur (a force-flip yields a key miss, not a conflict). It fails safe, but advertises protection it does not provide and leaves unreachable code that operators may trust when enabling the flag.
detail.recommendation: Either feed the FULL request payload (not the fingerprint subset) into the payload hash so same-key/different-payload conflicts can actually be detected, or remove the conflict/markResponseWithReceiptStoreConflict machinery and correct the comments to state that distinct logical requests always produce distinct keys.

findings.title: Replay can return a deleted memory id via the planner-save bypass

detail.evidence: The replay active-row query selects/compares content_hash only and never compares the live row id to the id in the cached response_payload. Canonical/planner saves skip the entire idempotency block (planner branch returns at 3636-3644 before the store at 3756), so a hard-delete (FK nulls receipt.memory_id at vector-index-schema.ts:668 while response_payload keeps the old id) followed by a planner re-create of identical content leaves receipt_A unrefreshed; a later direct save then matches content_hash and replays a response reporting the deleted id.
detail.why: A caller receives a success response whose data.id points at a memory that no longer exists; a follow-up memory_update by that id fails. The active-row recheck was added precisely to prevent stale replays but only guards content, not identity, and identity can diverge through the planner path.
detail.recommendation: Select id in the active-row query and honor the replay only when it equals extractMemoryIdFromResponse(lookup.response) (and/or the receipt's stored memory_id); otherwise drop the receipt and fall through. Apply the same id check in memory-crud-update.ts:262-267.

findings.title: Receipt TTL is enforced only at daemon boot

detail.evidence: pruneExpiredIdempotencyReceipts has a single caller, executed once during init; no periodic or per-write sweep exists (idempotency-receipts.ts:258-273 is otherwise uncalled in non-test code).
detail.why: These daemons are long-lived/warm; a daemon running longer than the 30-day TTL never sweeps, so receipts grow unbounded between restarts and rows past TTL persist. Staleness is mitigated by the active-row recheck, so impact is bounded growth plus a TTL guarantee weaker than the comment implies — relevant before turning the flag on for a persistent daemon.
detail.recommendation: Add a bounded periodic sweep (e.g. opportunistically on write or on a timer) in addition to the boot prune, or document that TTL only applies across restarts.

findings.title: Prune query cannot use the receipts index

detail.evidence: The only index is on (operation, created_at DESC) (vector-index-schema.ts:630-631) but prune filters WHERE updated_at < datetime('now', ?); no code path ever issues an UPDATE (storeIdempotencyReceipt uses ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING), so updated_at is immutable and equals created_at.
detail.why: The sweep does a full table scan for no behavioral gain; pruning on the indexed created_at would be identical and index-backed.
detail.recommendation: Prune on created_at (indexed), or add an index on updated_at if a future DO UPDATE path is intended.

findings.title: FTS5-fallback LIKE escaper omits backslash, diverging from canonical helper

detail.evidence: queryCausalEdgesLikeFallback escapes raw user query with `query.replace(/[%_]/g, '\\$&')` (only % and _) then binds `%${escaped}%` into `content_text LIKE ? ESCAPE '\\' OR title LIKE ? ESCAPE '\\'` (lines 273,278). The canonical escapeLikePattern() in vector-index-types.ts:29 escapes backslash FIRST (\\->\\\\) before % and _; this site does not.
detail.why: With ESCAPE '\\' active, a literal backslash in the query becomes a dangling escape introducer. If followed by a non-wildcard char, SQLite raises a runtime LIKE error (the .all() at line 282 is not wrapped in try/catch in the read excerpt, so it propagates); if at a wildcard boundary it silently alters matching. The epic's stated remediation was that all LIKE patterns route through one shared escaped helper — this hand-rolled escaper was missed.
detail.recommendation: Replace `query.replace(/[%_]/g, '\\$&')` with the shared escapeLikePattern(query) from vector-index-types.ts so backslash is neutralized consistently with every other LIKE site; keep the existing ESCAPE '\\' clause.

findings.title: Pre-fix RSS headline drifts between 687MB and 743MB

detail.evidence: changelog-002-010.md:23, parent spec.md:130, and before-vs-after.md:255/293 say the pre-fix realistic RSS spike was 743MB. The preserved 014 re-validation evidence says 686.8MB committed and 799.4MB batched in scratch/rss-probe-evidence.md:11-12, and 010 spec.md:66 repeats ~687MB. 010 implementation-summary.md:3 also says final 134MB while lines 50 and 91 say 136.5MB.
detail.why: The shipped benchmark story has multiple incompatible headline numbers. The pass/fail outcome is unchanged, but traceability is not clean enough for a benchmark-backed release note.
detail.recommendation: Choose the authoritative preserved benchmark number or add the missing 743MB artifact, then normalize the 010 changelog, parent spec, before-vs-after summary, and implementation-summary frontmatter/body to one pre-fix value and one final value.

findings.title: Inline false still enables trusted mode

detail.evidence: The parser derives rawFlag by stripping any =value at line 398, then the trusted/maintainer branch at lines 428-430 sets trusted=true without checking whether the token was --trusted=false, --trusted=0, or another inline value.
detail.why: Safety wrappers or generated CLI calls that serialize booleans as --trusted=false will unexpectedly grant mutation authority. This is especially risky because trusted mode gates shared-state mutation tools.
detail.recommendation: Reject inline values for --trusted/--maintainer, or parse --trusted=true|false with the same boolean parser used for --warm-only. Add tests proving --trusted=false and --trusted=0 do not satisfy the mutation gate.

findings.title: Fabricated non-canonical fingerprint on a completed packet evades validation and breaks continuity provenance

detail.evidence: session_dedup.fingerprint is 'sha256:1111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111' on a packet with completion_pct:100 and status:"completed". grep confirms 1111... is used only in scripts/test-fixtures/00{2,3,4}-valid-levelN/* — it is a test-fixture placeholder, not a runtime sentinel. Only ZERO_CONTINUITY_FINGERPRINT (all zeros) is recognized as 'never recorded' (spec-doc-structure.ts:32; continuity-freshness.ts:314).
detail.why: The value matches /^sha256:[a-f0-9]{64}$/ so SPECDOC_FRONTMATTER_005 (spec-doc-structure.ts:649) passes it silently and recursive --strict stays green, hiding the defect. But it is neither a real content fingerprint (cannot verify continuity) nor the canonical zero placeholder (so freshness will not skip it). When SPECKIT_COMPLETION_FRESHNESS=true, evaluateCompletionFreshness treats it as a usable stored fingerprint and compares it to the recomputed content hash (continuity-freshness.ts:324-325), producing a guaranteed false 'content_stale' signal. The completion record asserts a recorded continuity fingerprint that was never computed.
detail.recommendation: Replace the fabricated 'sha256:1111...' value with either the canonical zero placeholder (sha256:000...0) if no real fingerprint was captured, or a genuine buildContinuityFingerprint(content) value refreshed via the memory-save/completion workflow. Consider adding a validation rule that rejects known fixture-placeholder fingerprints (e.g. all-ones) in non-fixture spec folders so structural --strict catches this class.

findings.title: Top parent holds before-vs-after.md/timeline.md outside its own spec.md optional-doc allowlist; before-vs-after.md is a forbidden-class change narrative

detail.evidence: spec.md:87 names the permitted optional parent docs as exactly 'context-index.md migration bridge and resource-map.md'; spec.md:50-52 FORBIDS merge/migration/consolidation narratives at parent level. Yet `find -maxdepth 1` shows the parent root also contains before-vs-after.md (527 lines, a 'What Changed... Before/After/Impact' change narrative per its lines 1-60) and timeline.md (440 lines), plus handover.md — none named in the line 87 allowlist. validate.sh does not flag these, so it is undetected by tooling.
detail.why: The parent control doc's stated content discipline contradicts its actual contents: it forbids before/after consolidation narratives at parent level and enumerates a narrower optional-doc set than what exists. A reader trusting spec.md:87 to know the parent's docs is misinformed, and before-vs-after.md occupies a content class the same file declares forbidden — a self-inconsistent lean-trio/content-discipline state. Low severity: validator-tolerated, files are deliberately cross-referenced (context-index.md:7, handover.md:80), no behavior impact.
detail.recommendation: Either (a) relocate before-vs-after.md to a changelog rollup / child and keep only navigational bridges at the parent, or (b) if these are accepted reorg-bridge artifacts, update spec.md:87 to enumerate the full sanctioned parent-doc set (handover.md, context-index.md, resource-map.md, timeline.md, before-vs-after.md) and carve before-vs-after.md out of the FORBIDDEN content class at lines 50-52 so the discipline block stops contradicting the tree.

findings.title: '027 to 028 Split' current-folder column points to a nonexistent packet

detail.evidence: Lines 46/62 and the table at lines 50-60 resolve 9 moved Code Graph/CocoIndex phases to sibling packet '028-code-graph-and-cocoindex' (e.g. '005-cocoindex-complete-fork'). A full find over .opencode/specs returns no directory named 028-code-graph-and-cocoindex at any depth; the track's actual 028 packet is '029-memory-search-intelligence', which contains only .gitkeep + external/. Line 62 uses present-tense live guidance ('Use 028-code-graph-and-cocoindex for the moved ... children'), and the line-42 resolution note only covers 027-.../0NN- children, not this external packet.
detail.why: The doc's header (line 3) promises resolution to 'current homes', but this section's entire current-folder column is dangling — a reader/resume-agent locating a moved phase lands on a path that does not exist, with no forward pointer to its real fate.
detail.recommendation: Add a forward note recording what happened to 028-code-graph-and-cocoindex after the 2026-05-28 split (renamed/abandoned; current track 028 is 029-memory-search-intelligence), or explicitly mark the section as a historical event record and rephrase line 62 out of present-tense live guidance.

findings.title: Two divergent loop-lock CLI wrappers over one lib: deep-context drifted in flag contract and default TTL

detail.evidence: deep-context wrapper uses --lock/--packet/--owner (lines 60,86,88) with default ttlMs=3600000 (line 86); the runtime adapter scripts/loop-lock.cjs uses --lock-path/--packet-id/--owner-pid (lines 147-148,156) with default ttlMs=300000 (line 149). deep_context_auto.yaml:174 calls the workflows wrapper; deep_research/review/ai-council call the runtime adapter (verified via uniq of all acquire/release invocations). Neither acquire passes a TTL flag, so context locks live 1h vs 5min elsewhere. Both wrap lib/deep-loop/loop-lock.ts.
detail.why: The runtime adapter's own header (scripts/loop-lock.cjs:6-9) states it exists so callers 'share one locking contract instead of drifting per mode'; deep-context retaining a separate wrapper with different flag names and a 12x-different default TTL is exactly that drift. No current correctness break (each YAML matches its wrapper), but it is a maintenance hazard: an invocation copied between loops passes the wrong flag shape and silently fails (exit 3), and the stale-lock reclaim window differs across loops sharing the 'same source of truth' claim.
detail.recommendation: Point deep_context_auto/confirm.yaml at the runtime adapter (deep-loop-runtime/scripts/loop-lock.cjs) with --lock-path/--packet-id/--owner-pid, or align the deep-context wrapper's flag names and default TTL to the runtime adapter. If the host-driven loop genuinely needs in-process require (no re-exec) and a longer TTL, document that divergence explicitly in both wrapper headers so the difference is intentional, not accidental drift.

findings.title: Two GraphTraversal adapter methods are unused prod passthroughs that ignore instance database

detail.evidence: BetterSqliteGraphTraversal.collectWeightedWalk (lines 57-61) and collectDirectedReachability (lines 63-67) delegate straight to the imported module functions and never reference this.database. A grep across non-test/non-dist code finds production calls only to collectCausalWeightedNeighbors (causal-boost.ts:428) and collectDependencyReachability (memo.ts:223); the other two adapter methods have no production caller.
detail.why: Dead interface surface: instance methods that ignore instance state add maintenance weight and falsely imply the adapter owns those traversals, while the only value (db binding) is absent. They are reachable only via the contract test.
detail.recommendation: Either drop collectWeightedWalk/collectDirectedReachability from the GraphTraversal port (callers can use the bfs-traversal module functions directly, as they already do internally), or add a one-line comment documenting that they are reserved/contract-only — mirroring the explicit unadopted-port note already present in lexical-search.ts:58-65.

findings.title: Dead export: notifyStatediffSubscribers has zero callers anywhere

detail.evidence: `rg -nw notifyStatediffSubscribers` over the whole repo returns exactly one hit — the export at statediff.ts:230. No production caller and not even a test references it, unlike sibling exports planStatediff/applyStatediffActions which at least have statediff.vitest.ts coverage.
detail.why: A public exported async function with no caller and no test is unreachable code that adds maintenance surface and falsely implies a subscriber-notification path exists in the statediff reconciliation layer.
detail.recommendation: Delete notifyStatediffSubscribers, or wire it into applyStatediffActions if subscriber notification is actually intended; if kept as staged foundation, add a test and a comment noting it is not yet wired.

findings.title: Dead module: resolveEdgeTierBasement (edge-tier-basement.ts) only consumed by a test

detail.evidence: resolveEdgeTierBasement and the re-export EDGE_TIER_STATE_LIMITS are the module's only exports; the sole importer is feedback-retention-reducer.vitest.ts:8. The wired reducer feedback-retention-reducer.ts and memory-retention-sweep.ts (which DO import evaluateFeedbackRetention/recordFeedbackRetentionAudit) never import edge-tier-basement.
detail.why: The edge-floor retention decision the impl-summary (004-retention-reducer/implementation-summary.md:42,65) presents as built never runs against any production retention candidate — the helper is exercised only by unit tests, so the described causal-edge durability protection is not actually applied.
detail.recommendation: Wire resolveEdgeTierBasement into the retention sweep's edge path, or remove the module and the corresponding impl-summary claim if edge flooring is deferred.

findings.title: Dead export: codeHash() helper has no production call site

detail.evidence: `rg -n 'codeHash\('` shows the function invoked only in tests (canonical-fingerprint.vitest.ts:21-22, incremental-index-foundation.vitest.ts:30,76,91). Production incremental-index.ts imports canonicalFingerprint but not codeHash, and supplies input.codeHash from elsewhere (incremental-index.ts:745 reads input.codeHash, never calls the helper).
detail.why: An exported hashing helper intended for memo/code-version invalidation that no production path uses is dead code; it can drift from how production actually computes the codeHash value, creating a false sense the helper is the source of truth.
detail.recommendation: Either route production codeHash computation through this helper for consistency, or delete it and keep the tests on canonicalFingerprint.

findings.title: Dead export: flattenCompositeTarget is test-only and unused by the statediff planner

detail.evidence: `rg -nw flattenCompositeTarget` returns the definition (statediff.ts:169) and a single test reference (statediff.vitest.ts:56). It is not called by planStatediff (statediff.ts:132-168) nor any production handler that uses createStatediffAction.
detail.why: A public helper exported but never consumed by its own module's planner or production widens the API surface with no purpose and suggests an integration that does not exist.
detail.recommendation: Inline or remove flattenCompositeTarget, or have planStatediff use it if composite-target flattening is meant to be part of the plan path.

findings.title: Claude deep-review mirror Path Convention points to opencode runtime path, not its own

detail.evidence: Line 11 reads: **Path Convention**: Use only `.opencode/agents/*.md` as the canonical runtime path reference. Its two sibling Claude mirrors are correctly re-pointed: .claude/agents/deep-context.md:11 and .claude/agents/deep-research.md:11 both say `.claude/agents/*.md`. The opencode original (.opencode/agents/deep-review.md:28) and codex mirror (.codex/agents/deep-review.toml:15 = `.codex/agents/*.toml`) are each correct for their runtime — only this Claude mirror was not re-pointed during conversion.
detail.why: The Path Convention is a self-referential instruction naming the agent's own canonical spec directory. In the Claude runtime, deep-review is told its canonical path lives under .opencode, contradicting its actual location and diverging from both sibling Claude agents. It is a genuine mirror-conversion drift (a parity test grepping each .claude/agents/*.md for a .claude path reference would fail here). Impact is low — the agent reviews an external target rather than itself, so the loop does not break — but it is a traceability defect the prior multi-model pass missed.
detail.recommendation: Change .claude/agents/deep-review.md:11 to: **Path Convention**: Use only `.claude/agents/*.md` as the canonical runtime path reference. — matching its deep-context/deep-research Claude siblings. Add a parity assertion to the mirror generator/test so each runtime's Path Convention line names that runtime's own agent path.

findings.title: .claude @review Path Convention points at wrong runtime (.opencode instead of .claude)

detail.evidence: Line 11: "**Path Convention**: Use only `.opencode/agents/*.md` as the canonical runtime path reference." — but its four .claude siblings say `.claude/agents/*.md` (orchestrate.md:19, code.md:11, context.md:13, debug.md:11) and .codex/agents/review.toml:15 says `.codex/agents/*.toml`. .claude tally: 7 self-referential vs 2 wrong (review.md + deep-review.md). Wave A (2f52299b37) did not touch this line.
detail.why: The Path Convention steers the agent's canonical runtime path reference / exploration target. A Claude-runtime @review told to treat .opencode/agents as canonical mis-points at the wrong runtime mirror, breaking the otherwise-uniform per-runtime self-reference. It is a mirror-sync leftover (copied from opencode without re-pointing) missed by the prior deep-review whose byte-parity check predated the asymmetric Wave A edits.
detail.recommendation: Change `.opencode/agents/*.md` -> `.claude/agents/*.md` at .claude/agents/review.md:11; apply the same fix to .claude/agents/deep-review.md for full runtime parity.

findings.title: Codex mirror self-describes as 'orchestrate.md' inside the .toml

detail.evidence: §8 Orchestrator Self-Budget row reads 'This orchestrate.md file' but the runtime file is orchestrate.toml; the phrase is carried verbatim from the canonical .md.
detail.why: Cosmetic mirror-parity inaccuracy: a Codex-runtime self-reference points at a .md filename. It is a self-budget descriptor, not a dispatch template, so it cannot misroute a dispatch — hygiene only.
detail.recommendation: In the Codex mirror, change the row to 'This orchestrate.toml file' (or make it extension-neutral, e.g. 'this agent definition file') so each mirror's self-reference matches its own format.

findings.title: Evidence Group (§7) documented as a text envelope but no agent emits it and no text-parser feeds its validator

detail.evidence: §7 presents `AGENT_IO_EVIDENCE v1` as a `text`-fenced block in the same family as §2-§6 (all of which agents emit literally). But grep for AGENT_IO_EVIDENCE/claim_class/would_confirm across .opencode/.claude/.codex agents returns zero hits; the only consumer, post-dispatch-validate.ts:288/750, validates a structured JSONL `record.evidence` object via validateEvidenceContract(record.evidence) — never a parsed text envelope.
detail.why: A reader following the doc would format an AGENT_IO_EVIDENCE text block expecting it to be checked like its siblings, but the documented emittable surface and the actually-validated surface (a JSONL field written by deep-loop iterations) are disconnected, with no bridge between them. Mild traceability gap in an otherwise tightly-traced contract; does not block any exchange and is partly mitigated by the doc deferring schema ownership to deep-loop-runtime.
detail.recommendation: In §7, note explicitly that the Evidence Group is carried as the `evidence` field on a deep-loop JSONL iteration record (not as a free-standing AGENT_IO_EVIDENCE text envelope appended to agent output), or state that no agent currently emits it, so the documented representation matches the only consumer.

findings.title: spec.md internally contradicts itself on where Gate B lives; contradiction never reconciled

detail.evidence: NFR Maintainability (spec.md:209) and REQ-001 require predicates to 'live in one place (@orchestrate) so their scope cannot drift silently across agents', which the shared contract codifies (agent-io-contract.md:115: 'should not spread heuristic copies across leaf agents'). REQ-005 (spec.md:153) simultaneously requires sk-code/SKILL.md to carry the gate. Both are listed as satisfied P0s.
detail.why: The two requirements cannot both be literally true under the chosen centralized design, making REQ-005 unsatisfiable as written. The packet docs resolve it silently in code but never amend the spec, leaving a traceability artifact where a P0 acceptance criterion contradicts the implemented architecture.
detail.recommendation: Reconcile the spec: keep the centralized-predicate design and rewrite REQ-005/SC-002 to reference the orchestrator/contract surface (or an explicit sk-code Phase-0 reminder), removing the literal 'sk-code/SKILL.md requires...' wording that conflicts with REQ-001/NFR.

findings.title: Closure census does not reconcile with source-of-truth backlog and falsely claims all downgrades were triaged

detail.evidence: Census says 'Downgraded to P2 and triaged there | 63 (of which fixed/waived per P2 rules)'. The cited backlog actually has 57 downgraded entries (downgraded_p2=10 + downgraded_p2_v2=47, confirmed by grep+uniq), and only 49 of those 57 carry a p2_decision. The P1 census column sums to 51+24+63=138 against the stated 132 total; correct downgraded count 57 makes 51+24+57=132.
detail.why: In a verification-and-remediation packet whose deliverable is an honest finding ledger, the headline closure summary miscounts the downgrade bucket by 6, fails to sum to its own total, and asserts fixed/waived triage for 8 entries that were never triaged — misleading any operator/reviewer auditing closure completeness.
detail.recommendation: Correct the downgraded count to 57, fix the P1 column to sum to 132, and either triage the 8 untriaged downgrades with a p2_decision or soften the 'of which fixed/waived per P2 rules' claim to reflect that downgraded-but-deferred entries exist.

findings.title: Downgraded P1 finding closed with zero verification or disposition rationale (closest to silently-closed)

detail.evidence: Entry 'Comparison helper ignores the configured NDCG cutoff' (lines 1700-1715) carries disposition 'downgraded_p2' with no v2_proof and no p2_decision/p2_reason — unlike the other 56 downgrades. 7 of the 8 untriaged downgrades (240,275,684,1433,1452,1786,1803) at least record a v2_proof rationale; this one records nothing. The underlying code defect was independently resolved (bm25-baseline.ts:224,267 now thread configurable k into computeNDCG), but the registry gives no indication of verification or fix.
detail.why: A tracked P1 finding was de-prioritized out of the P1 fix/refute accounting with no recorded verification, fix, or waive rationale — the registry alone cannot tell whether it was resolved, deferred, or abandoned, violating the 'honest open status' / 'disposition is source of truth, nothing fixed unverified' contract (remediation-plan.md:14).
detail.recommendation: Add a v2_proof (citing bm25-baseline.ts:267 as resolved) and a p2_decision/p2_reason to entry idx for the NDCG-cutoff finding, and audit the other 7 untriaged downgrades for the same closure-rationale gap.

findings.title: Plan definition-of-done checkboxes do not match completed tasks

detail.evidence: plan.md Definition of Done boxes for iterations recorded, findings registry synthesized, and packet strict validation are unchecked, while tasks.md T007-T010 are all [x] and implementation-summary.md:79 reports strict validation pass.
detail.why: The plan does not reflect the actual done state, creating audit confusion.
detail.recommendation: Mark the Definition of Done checkboxes [x] or remove them if superseded by tasks.md.

findings.title: deep-research-config.json status still running

detail.evidence: deep-research-config.json has "status": "running"; implementation-summary.md and tasks.md state the program is complete.
detail.why: The program config does not match the completed execution state.
detail.recommendation: Update status to completed (or remove the stale field) to align with the final registry.

findings.title: Child spec.md continuity frontmatter never reconciled past scaffold

detail.evidence: spec.md frontmatter still has completion_pct: 0 (line 28), recent_action 'Populated spec/plan/tasks/impl-summary', next_safe_action 'design Unit A vector reconcile first' (line 18), while the same file's METADATA says Status 'Resolved' and impl-summary/tasks frontmatter say completion_pct 90. spec §3 also still quantifies Unit A as ~314 rows / 5 missing-success while tasks T004 + impl-summary record 91 rows actually reset.
detail.why: The completion rule requires reconciling _memory.continuity and spec.md status; a direct reader of spec.md's machine block would conclude the packet is 0% done with Unit A unstarted. Low operational impact only because the resume ladder reads continuity from implementation-summary.md (ADR-004).
detail.recommendation: Reconcile spec.md frontmatter (completion_pct, recent_action, next_safe_action) to the resolved state and update the §3 Unit A row counts to the implemented 91-row reality, or add a one-line 'implemented numbers differ' note.

findings.title: chore(027) catch-all snapshot lands ~18 non-027 spec trees + unverified refactor under one 027-attributed commit

detail.evidence: Commit 6ed0f30a2f 'chore(027): land full working-tree snapshot (operator-directed catch-all)' touches ~18 distinct spec trees outside 027 (skilled-agent-orchestration/122,126,134,138,140,143,144,146,147,148,149,150,151,152,153,155; system-spec-kit/026 and /028) plus the in-flight skill-advisor refactor + fable-mode tooling; the commit message states 'the in-flight advisor refactor was not independently verified here.'
detail.why: A single 027-attributed chore commit bundling work owned by ~18 other packets, including code the message admits was not verified, defeats per-epic git bisect/blame attribution and lands unverified changes in the tree — a maintainability/traceability cost. (Mitigated: it is transparently self-documented and operator-directed, and the underlying changes are individually owned by their real packets.)
detail.recommendation: Future bulk snapshots should be committed as a neutral chore (no per-epic scope prefix) or split by owning packet; explicitly tag any 'not independently verified' content so a later verification pass can find it. No action required on shipped state — the per-phase 027 work itself stayed within frozen scope.

findings.title: Stale phase-id TODO left in /doctor:update workflow asset

detail.evidence: Line 409 contains: // TODO(022): use resolveActiveProfileDbPath from shared/embeddings/profile.js for unambiguous active-profile selection when multiple provider DBs coexist.
detail.why: The new /doctor:update router loads this workflow asset. The inline TODO embeds a phase-id label (022) and leaves deferred work in code, violating the project's comment-hygiene rule. It also admits the context-index coverage step picks the first matching context-index__*.sqlite arbitrarily when multiple provider DBs coexist.
detail.recommendation: Resolve the active-profile DB selection (resolveActiveProfileDbPath now exists) and remove the inline TODO, or move the remaining work to a spec-doc task and replace the comment with a concise why.

findings.title: resolveExistingSpecFolderPath trusts caller-side validation for absolute paths (defense-in-depth gap)

detail.evidence: For absolute input, the function returns path.resolve(rawArg) when it exists with no validateFilePath/containment check; its result is the path that gets the canonical-save lock dir created (mkdirSync/writeFileSync at lines 464-465) and phase-parent metadata written (atomicWriteJson). Containment is enforced only earlier in main() via validateArguments()->isValidSpecFolder->isUnderApprovedSpecsRoot.
detail.why: Not currently exploitable — I confirmed the only callers (lines 852,868) run after validateArguments() in main(), so out-of-specs absolute paths are already rejected. But the write-sink resolver itself carries no containment guard, so a future caller invoking it without the gate would write outside the approved specs roots. It is the one resolver in this slice that does not defend itself.
detail.recommendation: Run the returned path through validateFilePath(resolved, getSpecsDirectories()) inside resolveExistingSpecFolderPath (and/or acquireCanonicalSaveLock) so containment is enforced at the write sink, independent of caller ordering.

round2CodeVerification.note: 16 code-defect P1s opposite-model refute-first; doc/meta P1s host-verified separately
